primary_diagnosis.name: Seropositive rheumatoid arthritis — acute flare (with low-grade fever; infection to be excluded)

pathophysiology_summary: Seropositive RA causes symmetric small-joint synovitis (MCP/PIP/MTP) with prolonged morning stiffness and systemic features; a viral illness can trigger a severe flare with low-grade fever and functional collapse. This mimics septic arthritis and systemic sepsis. Reassuring features are symmetric widespread involvement, a positive squeeze test, no single hot/red joint, and no high fever/rigors. However, a low-grade fever in a patient on an immunomodulator (hydroxychloroquine) mandates infection exclusion before immunosuppressive escalation, and telehealth cannot examine the joints or the squeeze test. The correct action is urgent in-person review with an infection screen and specialist-guided steroid/DMARD decisions — not de-novo steroids over chat — plus fall-safety for painful MTP weight-bearing, and urgent care for any single fiery joint or fever >38.5 with rigors.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetric MCP/PIP/MTP synovitis on established RA after a viral illness.
Fever + swollen joints in an immunomodulated patient demand septic-joint/infection screening.
Fever on immunomodulation must be taken seriously.
Post-viral onset overlaps; established RA distribution favours a flare.
No single hot joint, no high fever/rigors, positive squeeze test — an RA flare, but low-grade fever on immunomodulation needs infection exclusion.
Lower probability but not excludable without an exam/bloods.
Exclude with an infection screen before steroids.
Objective synovitis and fever, not soft-tissue tender points.

differential.diagnosis: Seropositive RA flare — infection to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: RA flare needing urgent in-person review, infection screen, specialist-guided steroids/DMARD, and fall-safety.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Mandates infection/septic-arthritis exclusion before immunosuppressive escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Masked/worsened infection if steroids started blindly.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming fiery red/hugely swollen, or fever >38.5 with rigors
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate septic arthritis/sepsis needing emergency care.

red_flags.description: High fall risk from MTP pain and stiffness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Painful weight-bearing with stiffness risks falls.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Starting de-novo prednisolone over chat in a febrile immunomodulated patient before excluding infection.
Attributing fever entirely to the flare without an infection screen.
Not addressing unsafe weight-bearing and mobility.
Not arranging specialist review for DMARD optimisation.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No single septic-looking joint; low-grade fever on immunomodulation — infection must be excluded in person
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person review and infection screen before steroids.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Assessment of gait/transfers and fall risk
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent in-person review with an infection screen and specialist-guided steroid/DMARD decisions; address fall safety; escalate to ED for a single fiery joint or fever >38.5 with rigors.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Starting steroids remotely could mask/worsen an infection in an immunomodulated patient; ignoring fall risk could lead to injury.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology (RA flare/acute hot joint)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A hot/acutely swollen joint or fever in an immunosuppressed RA patient requires infection exclusion before corticosteroid escalation.
Flares are managed with glucocorticoids and DMARD optimisation under specialist guidance once infection is excluded.
